text: Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon retracts the liver to reveal the stomach. The absence of a hiatal hernia is visually confirmed.

The surgeon uses a felt pen to draw precise lines on the stomach, marking the planned division points for the gastrectomy. A measuring tape is used to ensure accuracy, with the lower vertical line positioned five centimeters above the bottom of the stomach and the upper horizontal line marking the angularis. The marked area below the angularis is four centimeters wide, while above it, the width is planned to be two and a half to three centimeters.

The surgeon divides the attachments along the greater curve of the stomach, freeing the back wall of the stomach. The greater curve is visible on the right side of the screen, while the lesser curve is on the left. The anesthesiologist inserts a 36 French bougie tube into the distal stomach, preparing for gastric division.

The surgeon applies the Cavidian stapler with a black cartridge to the stomach, aligning it with the marked line to ensure a precise sleeve formation. The black cartridge is used for thicker stomach tissue at the bottom. The staple line is visible, maintaining equal distance from the lesser curve along both the back and front walls of the stomach. The surgeon fires the stapler, creating a consistent staple line, and proceeds with a second application using another black cartridge.

The surgeon's right hand holds the stapler while the assistant's hand, visible from the right, assists. The stapler is fired, creating a neat staple line along the edge of the gastric sleeve. The surgeon measures the upper part of the sleeve above the angularis, aiming for a width of two and a half to three centimeters.

The surgeon measures the stomach width to ensure it is two and a half to three centimeters wide above the angularis, aiming for a more aggressive sleeve construction for increased post-operative restriction. The purple line marks the desired width, and the surgeon demonstrates how pulling on the greater curve distorts the stomach, emphasizing the importance of precise stapler positioning to avoid a distorted sleeve.

The surgeon marks the upper part of the stomach with the bougie in place to identify the lesser curve, which is obscured by fat. The stapler is positioned along the purple line to create a sleeve approximately two and a half centimeters wide. The assistant holds the stomach to ensure precise stapler placement. The staple line appears straight and aligned with the lateral margin of the sleeve. The surgeon checks the width with the open jaw of the stapler, confirming it is two and a half centimeters wide, and fires the stapler again to complete the division.
The surgeon reinforces the top part of the staple line with sutures, visibly pulling the stomach tissue over the staple line to reduce tension and lower the risk of leaks.

The surgeon removes the gastrogrammat through the abdominal wall and performs a leak test by occluding the sleeve distally while the anesthesiologist injects air to distend the sleeve. The surgeon uses a tape measure to verify the dimensions of the sleeve, ensuring the upper part is between two and a half and three centimeters wide, with the staple line beginning four centimeters before the pylorus. The antrum is confirmed to be four centimeters wide. A final inspection shows no bleeding, no hiatal hernia, and a symmetric sleeve with a straight staple line. The liver retractor is removed, completing the gastric sleeve operation.
